LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AUG-22-2018 HEARING HELD ON PETITIONER SF URBAN FOREST COALITION'S MOTION FOR ATTORNEY FEES. AFTER ARGUMENT, THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. THE MOTION IS GRANTED IN PART. SFUFC IS AWARDED FEES AGAINST RESPONDENT SAN FRANCISCO COUNTY TRANSPORTATION AUTHORITY IN THE AMOUNT OF $75,000, CALCULATED AS 40 HOURS OF MR. GROSSMAN'S TIME AT THE HOURLY RATE OF $600, 90 HOURS OF MR. SEAVY'S TIME AT THE HOURLY RATE OF MR. SEAVY'S TIME OF $400, AND 60 HOURS OF MR. CHAFFEE'S TIME AT THE HOURLY RATE OF $150. ORDER SIGNED. (SEE ORDER FOR FULL DETAILS) JUDGE: HAROLD E. KAHN, CLERK: JACQUELINE ALAMEDA, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR 8600, 415.551.5868, EMAIL: TORREANOM@AOL.COM. (302/HEK)

calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorney Fees
Off Calendar Aug-22-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Charles K. Seavey, Esq. of The Law Offices of Charles K. Seavey, 415.365.7124, appearing for the Petitioner;
Natasha Saggar Sheth, Esq. of Nossaman LLP, 415.398.3600, appearing for the Defendants SF County Transportation Authority, Cynthia Fong, and Lilly Chang.

Ruling: ARGUED. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling as follows: Petitioner SF Urban Forest Coalition's motion for attorney fees is granted in part. SFUFC is awarded fees against respondent San Francisco County Transportation Authority in the amount of $75,000, calculated as 40 hours of Mr. Grossman's time at the hourly rate of $600, 90 hours of Mr. Seavy's time at the hourly rate of Mr. Seavy's time of $400, and 60 hours of Mr. Chaffee's time at the hourly rate of $150. The requested hourly rates for Mr. Grossman and Mr. Chaffee are reasonable and within the range for an attorney and a paralegal with their experience and skills. The $550 requested hourly rate for Mr. Seavy, who has been practicing law for only four years and appears to be a solo practitioner, is far in excess of what is within the reasonable range for an attorney with his skill and experience. $400 per hour is at the high end of the range for an attorney with Mr. Seavy's skill and experience. The recoverable hours claimed by SFUFC far exceeds what is reasonable for a fairly pedestrian California Public Records Act case, and taking into account that SFUFC did not prevail on the only arguably difficult and novel issue (the applicability of the Sunshine Ordinance) that was litigated. While the court does not doubt that the three timekeepers spent all the time they say they spent on this case, spending the time does not make the spent time recoverable. Excluding all time spent on the Sunshine Ordinance issues and all time spent after April 25, 2018 except for time on this motion and significantly reducing the hours spent on the core CPRA claim due to the unreasonableness of those hours and the fact that they included work on matters that went well beyond the relatively small number of documents sought by SFUFC on its pleaded CPRA claim, the court finds that the high end of recoverable time is 40 hours by Mr. Grossman, 90 hours by Mr. Seavy, and 60 hours by Mr. Chaffee. The significant reduction in the amount awarded from the amount sought is not intended, expressly or impliedly, as a criticism of the work of Mr. Grossman, Mr. Seavy and/or Mr. Chaffee, but merely that the great majority of the hours claimed by them and the hourly rate sought for the services of Mr. Seavy are far out of line for the success they achieved on SFUFC's CPRA claim. Order signed. (see order for full details)

Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR 8600, 415.551.5868, Email: torreanom@aol.com. =(302/HEK)

Hearing Re: Petition For Writ Of Mandamus
Off Calendar May-08-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Charles Seavey, Esq. 415-365-7124 for Petitioner SF Urban Forest Coalition.
Carl Blumenstein, Esq. 415-438-7219 of Nossman, llp for Defendant San Francisco County Transportation Authority.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares his views re: Chapter 5 of the Bay Area Bill. Petitioner argues 3 topics. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Hearing Re: Plaintiff's Petition For Writ Of Mandamus.

Petitioner SF Urban Forest Coalition's petition for writ of mandate is denied as to all three causes of action alleged by SFUFC. The portions of SFUFC's first and second causes of action seeking declaratory relief that the San Francisco County Transportation Authority is subject to the San Francisco Sunshine Ordinance lack merit. The SFCTA is an agency of the State of California and thus is exempt from a local ordinance such as the Sunshine Ordinance. Moreover, the language of the Sunshine Ordinance makes clear that the SFTCA is not covered by that Ordinance. SFUFC's argument that the Bay Area Bill provided only "the idea of possibly creating a county transportation agency" and the "framework" for such an agency is unpersuasive and not supported by either the text of the Bay Area Bill or analogous case law. Except for the portions of the first and second causes of action in SFUFC's petition seeking declaratory relief that the SFTCA is subject the Sunshine Ordinance, all of SFUFC's claims are moot because SFUFC has acknowledged that it has received all the documents that it requested in its petition. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Gordon Aiavao, CSR#11216, gaiavao@sftc.org, Reported. (D302/HK)

Hearing Re: Petition For Writ Of Mandamus
Off Calendar Apr-25-2018 Continued To May-08-18 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Charles K. Seavey of the law offices of Charles K. Seavey, LLC (415-365-7124) appears as counsel for petitioner, SF Urban Forest Coalition

Deputy City Attorney, Wayne K. Snodgrass of the San Francisco City Attorney (415-554-4675) appears as counsel for respondent, City and County of San Francisco

Attorney, Carl L. Blumenstein of the law offices of Nossman LLP (415-438-7219) appears as counsel for respondent, San Francisco County Transportation Authority

Court and counsel confer briefly as set forth in the transcript of the court reporter. Both sides submit without argument, except for a request for modification as to the continuance date. The Court confirms the tentative ruling with modification.

The hearing on the petition for writ of mandamus of petitioner as to the first and second causes of action is CONTINUED on the court's own motion to May 8, 2018 at 9:30 AM in Department 302 for ruling on the merits of those claims. While the court does not require any further briefing on the issue of whether respondent SFTCA is subject to the Sunshine Ordinance, the parties are permitted to provide short additional memorandum of no more than ten pages on that issue no later than May 2, 2018. The first and second causes of action are not moot due to the SFCTA's position that it is not subject to the Sunshine Ordinance, which is sufficient to establish an actual controversy per CCP 1060. (Environmental Defense Project of Sierra County v. County of Sierra (2008) 158 Cal. App. 4th 877; California Alliance for Utilities Safety and Education v. City of San Diego (1997) 56 Cal. App. 4th 1024).

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, e-mail: torreanom@aol.org (L&M302)

DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
Off Calendar Apr-25-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Charles K. Seavey of the law offices of Charles K. Seavey, LLC (415-365-7124) appears as counsel for petitioner/opposing party

Deputy City Attorney, Wayne K. Snodgrass of the San Francisco City Attorney (415-554-4675) appears as counsel for respondent/moving party, City and County of San Francisco

Attorney, Carl L. Blumenstein of the law offices of Nossman LLP (415-438-7219) appears as counsel for respondent, San Francisco County Transportation Authority

Court and counsel confer briefly as set forth in the transcript of the court reporter. Both sides submit without argument.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

RESPONDENT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION is OVERRULED IN PART and SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND in part. As the parties have resolved all issues relating to the third cause of action, the demurrer of respondents SFCTA, Tily Chang and Cynthia Fong is overruled as to the first and second causes of action and sustained without leave to amend as to the third cause of action. The first and second causes of action are not moot due to the SFCTA's position that it is not subject to the Sunshine Ordinance, which is sufficient to establish an actual controversy per CCP 1060. (Environmental Defense Project of Sierra County v. County of Sierra (2008) 158 Cal. App. 4th 877; California Alliance for Utilities Safety and Education v. City of San Diego (1997) 56 Cal. App. 4th 1024).

Written order is signed and filed.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, e-mail: torreanom@aol.org (L&M302)
